kern/syscall/execv_syscall.c

Go to the documentation of this file.
00001 /*
00002  * execv_syscall.c
00003  *
00004  *  Created on: May 16, 2010
00005  *      Author: Luigi Grimaudo  153740
00006  *                      Piera Gueli             160119
00007  */
00008 
00009 
00010 #include <types.h>
00011 #include <kern/unistd.h>
00012 #include <kern/errno.h>
00013 #include <lib.h>
00014 #include <addrspace.h>
00015 #include <thread.h>
00016 #include <current.h>
00017 #include <array.h>
00018 #include <syscall.h>
00019 #include <vnode.h>
00020 #include <spl.h>
00021 #include <kern/fcntl.h>
00022 #include <copyinout.h>
00023 #include <mips/trapframe.h>
00024 
00025 /* execv replaces the currently executing program with a newly loaded program image. This occurs within one process; the process * id is unchanged. The pathname of the program to run is passed as program. The args argument is an array of 0-terminated *strings. 
00026 * The array itself should be terminated by a NULL pointer. The argument strings should be copied into the new process as the new * process's argv[] array. In the new process, argv[argc] must be NULL.
00027 * By convention, argv[0] in new processes contains the name that was used to invoke the program. This is not necessarily the    *same as program, and furthermore is only a convention and should not be enforced by the kernel.
00028 * The process file table and current working directory are not modified by execve. 
00029 */
00030 
00031 
00032 int sys_execv(const char *program, char **args)
00033 {
00034         struct vnode *v;
00035         vaddr_t entrypoint, stackptr;
00036         int result,errno;
00037 
00038         /* Kernel layer */
00039         int p_argc;
00040         char **p_args;
00041         size_t copystrlen;
00042 
00043         /* User layer */
00044         char **new_args;
00045         int i, len, total_len;
00046 
00047         /* Find the number of arguments */
00048         i = 0;
00049         p_argc = 0;
00050         while (args[i++] != NULL) {
00051                 p_argc++;
00052         }
00053         p_argc++;
00054 
00055 
00056         /* Copy args in user space to kernel space */
00057         p_args = (char **)kmalloc(sizeof(char *) * (p_argc + 1));
00058         for (i = 1; i < p_argc; i++) {
00059                 p_args[i] = (char *)kmalloc(sizeof(char)*strlen(args[i-1]));
00060 
00061                 copyinstr((userptr_t)args[i-1], p_args[i], strlen(args[i-1])+1, &copystrlen); 
00062         }
00063         
00064         p_args[0] = (char *)kmalloc(sizeof(char)*strlen(program));
00065         copyinstr((userptr_t)program, p_args[0], strlen(program)+1, &copystrlen); 
00066         
00067         p_args[p_argc] = NULL;
00068         
00069         
00070 
00071         /* Open the executable file */
00072         result = vfs_open((char *)program, O_RDONLY, 0,&v);
00073         if (result) {
00074                 errno = result;
00075                 return errno;
00076         }
00077 
00078         /* Because it is user program, t_addrspace should not be NULL.*/
00079         KASSERT(curthread->t_addrspace != NULL);
00080         as_destroy(curthread->t_addrspace);
00081         curthread->t_addrspace = as_create();
00082         if (curthread->t_addrspace == NULL) {
00083                 vfs_close(v);
00084                 errno = ENOMEM;
00085                 return errno;
00086         }
00087         /* Activate it */
00088         as_activate(curthread->t_addrspace);
00089 
00090         /* Load the executable. */
00091         result = load_elf(v, &entrypoint);
00092         if (result) {
00093                 /* thread_exit destroys curthread->t_addrspace */
00094                 vfs_close(v);
00095                 errno = result;
00096                 return errno;
00097         }
00098 
00099         /* Done with the file now. */
00100         vfs_close(v);
00101 
00102         /* Define the user stack in the address space */
00103         result = as_define_stack(curthread->t_addrspace, &stackptr);
00104         if (result) {
00105                 errno = result;
00106                 return errno;
00107         }
00108         
00109 
00110         /* Calculate total length of memory buffer for ensure that
00111          * data is properly aligned. They must start
00112          * at an address that is divisible by 4.
00113          */
00114         total_len = 0;
00115         for (i = 0; i < p_argc; i++) {
00116                 len = ((int)strlen(p_args[i]) / 4 + 1) * 4;
00117                 total_len += len;
00118         }
00119         
00120         /* Address of the args in user space */
00121         new_args = (char**)(stackptr - total_len - (p_argc + 1)*4 - STACK_OFFSET);
00122 
00123         /* Copy the args aligned in user space */
00124         new_args[p_argc] = NULL;
00125         len = 0;
00126         for (i = p_argc - 1; i > -1; i--) {
00127                 len += ((int)strlen(p_args[i]) / 4 + 1) * 4;
00128                 new_args[i] = (char*) ( stackptr - len - STACK_OFFSET);
00129 
00130                 copyout(p_args[i], (userptr_t)new_args[i], strlen(p_args[i]));
00131         }
00132 
00133         
00134         /* Free kernel buffer memory */
00135         for (i = 0; i < p_argc; i++) {
00136                 kfree(p_args[i]);
00137         }
00138         kfree(p_args);
00139         
00140         
00141         enter_new_process(p_argc/*argc*/,  (userptr_t)new_args/*userspace addr of argv*/,
00142                           stackptr, entrypoint);
00143 
00144 
00145 
00146         /* enter_new_process does not return */
00147         panic("enter_new_process returned\n");
00148         return EINVAL;
00149 }
00150
